perturbation.original_text: The validity, construction and performance of this Agreement shall be governed by the laws, without regard to the laws as choice or conflict of laws, of the state of California.
perturbation.changed_text: The validity, construction and performance of this Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the state of Nevada, without regard to its laws as choice or conflict of laws.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from California to Nevada introduces a contradiction if the parties, subject matter, or performance of the contract have significant connections to California. California choice-of-law rules, under certain circumstances, may still apply even with a foreign governing law clause.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1646 states that a contract is to be interpreted according to the law and usage of the place where it is to be performed, or, if it does not indicate a place of performance, according to the law and usage of the place where it is made. If the contract is performed in California, or made in California, then this clause contradicts California law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1646 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1646. A contract is to be interpreted according to the law and usage of the place where it is to be performed; or, if it does not indicate a place of performance, according to the law and usage of the place where it is made. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the governing law from California to Nevada. The Perturbation Explanation indicates this creates a contradiction if the contract's performance has connections to California, as California choice-of-law rules might still apply. The Law Explanation states that California Civil Code Section 1646 dictates that a contract is interpreted based on the law of the place of performance or where it was made. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly quotes California Civil Code Section 1646, stating a contract is to be interpreted according to the law and usage of the place where it is to be performed or made. Therefore, if the contract is performed or made in California, changing the governing law to Nevada directly contradicts California law, as the contract should be interpreted under California law. This change alters a core legal obligation, making the contract potentially unenforceable under California law, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In the event of a breach, or threatened breach of any provision of this Agreement, the non-breaching party shall give the breaching party a notice to cure within three (3) business days.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of a breach, or threatened breach of any provision of this Agreement, the non-breaching party shall give the breaching party a notice to cure within ten (10) calendar days.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the cure period from 'three business days' to 'ten calendar days' alters a critical timeframe. This could conflict with statutory or common law that implies or requires a 'reasonable' cure period, depending on the nature of the breach and the industry.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Commercial Code Section 2309(1) states that the time for shipment or delivery or any other action under a contract if not otherwise agreed shall be a reasonable time. Changing the cure period affects 'any other action' and the reasonableness of the new time could be contested. If a reasonable time, considering industry standards, is less than 10 calendar days, this clause conflicts with the law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, COM 2309 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Commercial Code - COM DIVISION 2. SALES [2101 - 2801] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 3. General Obligation and Construction of Contract [2301 - 2328] ( Chapter 3 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 2309. (1) The time for shipment or delivery or any other action under a contract if not provided in this division or agreed upon shall be a reasonable time. (2) Where the contract provides for successive performances but is indefinite in duration it is valid for a reasonable time but unless otherwise agreed may be terminated at any time by either party. (3) Termination of a contract by one party except on the happening of an agreed event requires that reasonable notification be received by the other party and an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if its operation would be unconscionable. (Enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the cure period from 'three business days' to 'ten calendar days'. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation state this change could conflict with the legal requirement that the cure period be 'reasonable', depending on industry standards. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically COM 2309(1), states that 'the time for shipment or delivery or any other action under a contract if not provided in this division or agreed upon shall be a reasonable time.' Because the altered timeframe might not be 'reasonable' under the law, the changed contract creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties shall use the Confidential Information only for the purpose of evaluating a potential business, employment and/or investment relationship with the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties shall use the Confidential Information for any purpose they deem fit, including evaluating a potential business, employment and/or investment relationship with the other party.
perturbation.explanation: By allowing the use of confidential information for 'any purpose they deem fit,' the contract contradicts the fundamental principle of a non-disclosure agreement, which restricts the use of such information. This could violate trade secret laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) - Cal. Civ. Code § 3426
perturbation.law_explanation: The CUTSA protects trade secrets from misappropriation. Allowing a party to use confidential information for 'any purpose' directly conflicts with the purpose of protecting trade secrets, making the agreement potentially unenforceable under this Act.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text, by allowing the use of confidential information for 'any purpose they deem fit,' directly contradicts the purpose of protecting trade secrets. The original text restricts the use of confidential information to evaluating a potential business relationship. The changed text broadens this scope to 'any purpose,' which undermines the core principle of confidentiality. Snippet 1 cites the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), implying the existence of trade secret protections. By permitting the use of confidential information for any purpose, the modified text creates a clear path for misappropriation, directly conflicting with the intent of CUTSA as highlighted in the Law Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: All notices hereunder shall be in writing and shall be deemed to have been duly given if delivered personally, one day after delivery to a nationally recognized overnight delivery service, charges prepaid, or three days after being sent by registered or certified mail, postage prepaid, to the parties at their respective addresses set forth above
perturbation.changed_text: All notices hereunder may be given orally and shall be deemed to have been duly given immediately if delivered personally, or one week after emailing the parties, to their respective addresses set forth above.
perturbation.explanation: This changes the notice requirements from mandatory written notice to allowing oral notice, and changes the delivery terms from registered/certified mail to email, which is less reliable and verifiable. This contradicts established legal standards for contract notification, particularly in business dealings where written records are crucial.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1010
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1010 states that notices must be in writing. By allowing oral notices, the contract directly contradicts this provision.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1010 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 5. Notices, and Filing and Service of Papers [1010 - 1020] ( Chapter 5 enacted 1872. ) 1010. Notices must be in writing, and the notice of a motion, other than for a new trial, must state when, and the grounds upon which it will be made, and the papers, if any, upon which it is to be based. If any such paper has not previously been served upon the party to be notified and was not filed by him, a copy of such paper must accompany the notice. Notices and other papers may be served upon the party or attorney in the manner prescribed in this chapter, when not otherwise provided by this code. No bill of exceptions, notice of appeal, or other notice or paper, other than amendments to the pleadings, or an amended pleading, need be served upon any party whose default has been duly entered or who has not appeared in the action or proceeding. (Amended by Stats. 1935, Ch. 722.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1010, as stated in the Law Explanation. The original contract mandates written notices, while the changed text allows oral notices. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states: 'Notices must be in writing'. Allowing oral notices is a clear violation of this requirement. Therefore, the modification results in a clear non-compliance with the law.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement contains the entire understanding between the Parties concerning the disclosure of Confidential Information and supersedes any prior agreements, understandings, or representations with respect thereto.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement contains the partial understanding between the Parties concerning the disclosure of Confidential Information and does not supersede any prior agreements, understandings, or representations with respect thereto.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'entire understanding' to 'partial understanding' and stating that the agreement 'does not supersede' prior agreements directly undermines the purpose of an integration clause, which aims to consolidate all agreements into a single document. This creates ambiguity and could lead to disputes over which agreements are actually binding.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1856
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1856 codifies the parol evidence rule, which generally prevents the introduction of extrinsic evidence to contradict or vary the terms of a completely integrated written agreement. This change directly contradicts the intent and effect of a standard integration clause, allowing prior agreements to potentially override the current agreement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1856 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 4. MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [1855 - 2107] ( Heading of Part 4 amended by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) TITLE 1. OF THE GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF EVIDENCE [1855 - 1866] ( Title 1 enacted 1872. ) 1856. (a) Terms set forth in a writing intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to the terms included therein may not be contradicted by evidence of a prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement. (b) The terms set forth in a writing described in subdivision (a) may be explained or supplemented by evidence of consistent additional terms unless the writing is intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement. (c) The terms set forth in a writing described in subdivision (a) may be explained or supplemented by course of dealing or usage of trade or by course of performance. (d) The court shall determine whether the writing is intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to the terms included therein and whether the writing is intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement. (e) Where a mistake or imperfection of the writing is put in issue by the pleadings, this section does not exclude evidence relevant to that issue. (f
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes 'entire understanding' to 'partial understanding' and negates the superseding effect of the agreement. This contradicts the parol evidence rule, specifically California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1856(a), as it allows prior agreements to potentially contradict the current agreement. The snippet states that 'Terms set forth in a writing intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to the terms included therein may not be contradicted by evidence of a prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement.' By changing the agreement to reflect a 'partial understanding' that does not supersede prior agreements, the modified text directly violates this provision, creating a legal contradiction.
